p25.li_context: Desmond Oathes and an incredible team at the University of Pennsylvania is now out in Advanced Science: "Personalized Network-Guided Neuromodulation Enhances Human Working Memory" In this work, we took a different approach and let each persons brain help set the parameters for stimulation. Using concurrent TMS and fMRI, along with a brain-state decoder, we built an adaptive framework that (1) identifies an individualized stimulation target within each participants personalised functional networks, (2) Uses a real-time neural decoder to track how their working memory networks respond to stimulation, (3) empirically selects the optimal stimulation frequency for that specific individual, and (4) tested whether decoder-defined optimal frequency actually modulated working memory more than a suboptimal frequency in a separate task across repeated sessions. We found that when TMS was delivered at the decoder-identified optimal frequency over multiple sessions, participants showed reliable improvements in working memory performance. The very same individuals showed no such improvement when they received their suboptimal frequency, even though we stimulated the exact same personalized brain target. The effect was also specific to working memory: performance on a separate processing-speed control task did not change, suggesting we were modulating working memory networks rather than just making people respond faster in general. Overall, this study provides a proof of concept for a more principled, biomarker-driven approach to cognitive neuromodulation, one that personalizes both where and how we stimulate, with potential implications for cognitive enhancement and future clinical applications. Grateful to the fantastic team of collaborators who made this project possible. Ted Satterthwaite, Christos Davatzikos, Yong Fan, Camille Blaine, and others.

r23.li_context: There are two important things happening in AI right now, and nobody has put them together. At the cutting edge, agent builders are converging on the same discovery: the simplest way to give an agent durable memory is a markdown wiki. At the enterprise end of the scale, Databricks, Snowflake, Google, Microsoft and Amazon are all converging on semantic layers, context graphs and ontologies to ground their agents. The thing that will seem obvious in retrospect - but remember, you heard it here first - is that these do not need to be separate ideas: You can combine a markdown wiki with a formal knowledge graph and ontology Psychologists distinguish episodic memory - what happened - from semantic memory - what things mean. Markdown is a beautiful substrate for the first: meetings, decisions, plans, projects, notes, episodes. Ontology supplies the second: identity, type, relationship, constraint and meaning. We've spent just over a year running our company on exactly this combination. It is an extremely powerful pattern. Now we're publishing it as an open standard for the community to evolve. It's called Vault-LD. The idea is simple: markdown plus YAML-LD frontmatter. Markdown is the prompt - the text the model reads. YAML is the structure - typed fields a machine can query. LD is the meaning - linked data connecting those fields back to a formal ontology. Prose, structure and semantics in one file, each layer optional until you need it. The web has already become a distributed global knowledge graph by adding islands of JSON-LD to HTML pages. Vault-LD applies the same move to agent memory: YAML-LD frontmatter at the top of markdown files. A note can begin as ordinary prose. Then it can gain typed fields. Then those fields can link to shared meaning. Then the whole vault can project into a graph, where inference, validation, query and integration are already waiting. From there, vaults can be exposed as DPROD data products, which means the pattern scales from a private second brain to team memory, from team memory to enterprise integration, and from enterprise integration towards an open, decentralised Agentic Web. Ontology is how agent memory grows up. Agent memory is how ontology goes everywhere. The Simplest Thing That Could Possibly Work:[ https://lnkd.in/eWmMmm_7 <https://www.knowledge-graph-guys.com/blog/knowledge-graphs-are-going-mainstream-the-new-foundation-for-ai>

d41.li_context: Mainstream neuroscience continues to treat the vagus nerve as a simple toggle for the parasympathetic system. This reductionism misses the reality of a bi-directional architecture that dictates systemic inflammatory states and organ-level signaling long before the brain processes a feeling of calm. When you engage the auricular branch or manipulate breath patterns, you are not just relaxing; you are accessing a structural gateway through the jugular foramen that connects the brainstem directly to the heart, lungs, and gut. The common mistake is viewing vagal tone as a temporary state rather than a physiological capacity. The nerves extensive branching allows it to simultaneously regulate heart rate variability and the cholinergic anti-inflammatory pathway. It is a physical bridge between the amygdala and the abdominal viscera. Stimulating the ventral vagal complex does more than suppress a stress response; it facilitates a shift in cellular priority from defense to repair. Localized interventions like ear-based stimulation or extended exhales work because the nerve is a singular, wandering entity with multiple entry points. The effects spread across the gut-brain axis because the anatomy demands it, not because of a vague mind-body connection. Lasting resilience requires weeks of consistent signaling to force neural pathways to adapt. We need to stop talking about hacking the vagus and start recognizing it as the primary physiological substrate for systemic homeostasis and social engagement. <https://www.skool.com/vagus/classroom/4d7222e2?md=dac8a6895f51453581462981f4ee242d> The research in AI is by definition multi-disciplinary. You cannot remove the Biology from the inspiration, the Physics from its execution, and deep Mathematics from its foundation. In October 2024, the FlyWire Consortium (led by Princeton, Harvard, and Janelia Research Campus) published the first-ever complete synapse-level map of an adult fruit fly brain (Drosophila melanogaster), detailing 139,255 neurons and over 50 million synaptic connections. Scientists simulated the fruit flys entire brain and let it control a virtual body. It learned to walk on its own. Studies like this help answer questions like, (1) Is the biological brain anywhere near how the LLM/World Models look today ? (2) How did the nature evolve a living brain with hundreds of neurons in roundworms to billions of neurons in humans ? (3) How do the simplest of organism reason ? <https://flywire.ai/> <https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39533006/> <https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.16792> 1w : Dr. Miguel Nicolelis is widely regarded as one of the pioneers of the brainmachine interface (BMI) field. Beginning in 1989, he and his collaborators developed technologies capable of simultaneously recording the activity of multiple neurons in freely moving animals. By 1998, the team had achieved synchronous recordings from hundreds of neurons, and in 2000, Nicolelis formally introduced the term brainmachine interface. Nicolelis argues that BMI is a broader concept than braincomputer interface (BCI), as it encompasses communication pathways between the brain and a wide range of mechanical and electronic devices, rather than just computers. At the level of basic neuroscience, Nicolelis work helped bring neuronal ensemble theory back to the forefront of the field. Through the real-time decoding of neural signals, his team demonstrated that the cerebral cortex operates as an integrated network rather than a collection of isolated functional regions. Their experiments enabled animals to control robotic arms using only neural activity and revealed remarkable neuroplasticity, showing that the brain can incorporate external tools into its own body representation. Today, Nicolelis is exploring the concept of a Brainet, investigating the mechanisms of cortical synchronization among individuals within social groups. He believes that such neural synchronization may serve as a fundamental basis for cohesive social behavior.

d48.li_context: This alert on aggregating XAI-based explanations to uncover spectral-spatial patterns in CNN EEG models points to a shift: explainability isnt just a compliance checkbox - it can be a discovery engine. If you aggregate explanations across patients, sessions, and labels, youre no longer asking why did the model predict this? Youre asking what signal does the model believe exists in the biology? That matters because EEG pipelines are notoriously fragile: artifacts, leakage, and spurious correlations can look like accuracy. Aggregated XAI can help separate real neurophysiology from lab-specific noise and highlight consistent frequency-band and region patterns you can validate experimentally. Done right, it becomes a bridge between black-box performance and scientific credibility. My controversial view: most XAI for healthcare today is theater - pretty heatmaps that comfort humans while silently laundering bad causal stories. If your explanations cant survive aggregation, perturbation, and cross-site replication, theyre not explanations - theyre UI. Best practice: combine aggregated XAI with ablations, artifact controls, and out-of-distribution testing. d50.li_context: So if you asked me "does depression cause insulin resistance or does insulin resistance cause depression?", being the smart aleck I am, I'd just answer: "Yes." Insulin resistance can lead to cognitive impairment, cortisol can contribute to hyperinsulinemia, of course depression can contribute to both hypo and hyperphagia, low activity, and of course the medications that get used end up contributing too. Sabrina Wong, Roger McIntyre, and colleagues just tried to pin down that second half in the Journal of Affective Disorders. Systematic review, 19 trials, about 1,200 patients with major depression, asking what specific antidepressants do to validated measures of insulin sensitivity. Also interesting that whether or not the patient responds to the medication matters for the metabolic effect, say for instance, improved insulin sensitivity was associated with amitriptyline, but only in remitters. Now did the insulin resistance get better because amitriptyline, or because someone's depression got better and they started exercising? Caveat: evidence here is thin, there just isn't that much out there, this review is nineteen small studies, and while the authors were (admirably!) looking for things like fasting insulin and HOMA-IR, it just isn't done that much and most of the insulin resistance data is downstream glucose and HbA1c. So I wouldn't say the takeaway is "avoid drug X." It's that the metabolics can't really be separated from the condition. A fasting glucose and insulin gets you HOMA-IR, and almost nobody orders it. Treat the depression to response, watch the metabolism, and stop pretending the two are separate problems. <https://mh2.health/> <https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0165032726010086?via%3Dihub>

d54.li_context: Do you think you know the default mode network? The DMN is the most energetically demanding brain circuit in humans. It is constantly active when you are resting, yet it also activates in understanding self, others (empathy), and is the seat of neural processing of self-identity. It is chronically elevated in depression - as a well-understood hallmark of that condition. It deeply deactivates the salience network - reducing our monitoring of our environment. While widely associated with large cortical areas, with new multi-echo fMRI capabilities, a beautiful constellation of core modulatory deep-brain regions become star players in the control of the DMN. These include the bed nucleus stria terminalis (BNST, responsible for anxiety, stress, and hormonal modulation), peri-aqueductal gray (PAG, responsible for pain perception and opiate receptors), ventral tegmentum (VTa, key for dopamine signaling), and so many other vital deep brain control regions rarely considered in DMN conversations. Why is this important? These maps show how our lived experience as human beings connects to our brain's biology and how it's affected by the environment, medicines, and our bodies. <https://www.eyethree.ai/> "Do I need a neuroscience degree to build EEG apps?" d70.li_context: Hot off the press today in Cell Press "Objective quality assessment for precision functional MRI data" from a collaboration led by Charles Lynch at Weill Cornell Medicine and including some folks from our group Derrick Matthew Buchanan PhD Mahendra Bhati and Nolan Williams. We're passionate about the thesis and power of N of 1 precision brain imaging for neuropsychiatry, but it has to be done right. This new paper provides a novel model benchmark for this. Precision functional mapping (PFM) enables the individual-level characterization of brain network organization but requires substantially more and higher-quality fMRI data than is standard. Despite the growing use of PFM, the objective criteria for data sufficiency and the quality needed to ensure interpretable and replicable individual-level results remain unclear. Here, we introduce the network similarity index (NSI), an objective measure of the extent to which functional connectivity (FC) patterns express the large-scale network structure required for PFM. The NSI captures low-spatial-frequency, coherent network organization and denoising fidelity, and it aligns closely with blinded expert assessments of PFM usability. The NSI also accounts for the variability in the rate at which FC becomes reliable across individuals. This NeuroResource provides an open source framework for NSI-based data quality evaluation and models linking NSI values with expert-judged PFM suitability. This framework can inform expected returns from additional data collection, thus enabling principled decisions about data sufficiency and replication in precision fMRI research. Congrats to all of the authors who made this happen! d71.li_context: Out now in Nature Mental Health: Moving beyond accuracy to overcome translational barriers in depression relapse prediction But simply chasing higher accuracy may not be the way forward. First, many believe our "best" models rely on MRI, genomics, or other resource-intensive data, which restricts their utility to specialized centers and research settings. So the question is: what decrease in model accuracy are we willing to accept in order to facilitate broader implementation? Second, and perhaps more importantly, we rarely ask what level of prediction performance is actually useful in clinical practice. The figure illustrates this problem: two models can have exactly the same AUC, yet lead to very different clinical consequences. One misses many patients who will relapse. The other identifies more relapses but triggers many unnecessary interventions (think of maintenance ECT for instance). The key question is therefore not whether we can increase AUC from 0.70 to 0.72. It is: How many unnecessary interventions are we willing to accept to prevent one relapse? Until psychiatric prediction research starts evaluating clinical utilitynot just predictive accuracywe risk optimizing metrics rather than improving patient care. Great work with Nils R. r3.li_context: I am talking to a lot of clinicians that are eager to use AI to augment themselves and their practice, but they don't have BAAs in place with the model providers they want to use or they want to be very careful about where PHI is shared. They smartly turn to de-identification (), but find the results aren't as useful because a lot of patient context is lost in the process. Take this example: Original PHI-containing line: Liam Carter, DOB 6/1/2026, was seen in the ED on 6/18/2026 after his mother reported a rectal temperature of 100.8F. What's the workup? Traditional deid/redaction: \[NAME\], DOB \[DATE\], was seen in the ED on \[DATE\] after his \[FAMILY MEMBER\] reported a rectal temperature of 100.8F. What's the workup? What gets lost: The LLM no longer knows this is a 17-day-old newborn, a critical age for that fever. What's needed here is what is called decontextualization (). Now our friend Liam's data can show up like this after going through decon: A 17-day-old newborn was seen in the ED after a caregiver reported a rectal temperature of 100.8F. What's the workup? The answer is going to be very different compared to the deid query and response, because decon can preserve the clinical context to get the most out of raw LLM outputs that are very context dependent. What's great is you can create a way to do this even on a laptop that is totally local, no cloud, no API keys, no cost. With a little help from your favorite agentic agent, a regex table (a list of fixed PHI direct identifiers \[name\] etc) and very small local model from OpenMed, you can create your own decon pipeline. Then you can take text that contains PHI, run it through your local decon pipeline first, review the transformed output, and use the decontextualized version with AI tools - reducing the chance that PHI leaves your computer or clinical system while preserving the clinical context the model needs. This doesnt replace HIPAA policies or your validation, but its a practical starting point. Want a jumpstart - feel free to fork or clone my own work on it that I share with clinicians and modify for your use case (I'll assume you'll ask Claude or Codex for some help too) <https://huggingface.co/OpenMed/OpenMed-PII-SuperClinical-Large-434M-v1> I've seen too many patients spend too much time trying to find a therapist, not make progress in therapy or not be able to afford treatment for anxiety, so I dug into this a bit and found a good comparison of different treatments for Generalized Anxiety Disorder recently published in JAMA, Journal of the American Medical Association. I was surprised by the results and wrote this article summarizing how treatment lines up with the current neuroscience understanding of anxiety. I also added some pragmatic suggestions for people who are looking for anxiety treatment (and therapists). <https://judbrewer.substack.com/p/why-psychotherapy-may-not-be-the?r=e03d2&utm_campaign=post&utm_medium=web&triedRedirect=true> \*\*\*Just Published\*\*\* Our latest paper just published in the Journal of Personality and Social Psychology. Led by Tomiko Yoneda and Eileen Graham, this registered report was a significant undertaking to say the least. We arrived at the research questions from multiple perspectives, statistical approaches, etc., in 11 longitudinal studies from 18 countries (175,070 people) who were followed across large number of multiple waves of data across adulthood in each. A few more specific findings from the study: (1) A slight 10% increase in an individuals reported \#loneliness is associated with an 8% to 9% increase in the risk of shifting from healthy cognitive function into mild or severe cognitive impairment. (2) Loneliness also seems to reduce the likelihood of a person reversing mild cognitive impairment and returning to normal \#brain function by 3%. Conclusion is that loneliness really does appear to be consistently associated with an elevated risk of cognitive impairment and mortality risk across statistical approaches, even after adjusting for other important factors, like social isolation (e.g., involvement in groups, degree of social connection etc). Conversely social isolation was not consistently associated with cognitive impairement and showed weaker associations with mortality risk. Conclusion: Loneliness is a robust proximal predictor of major aging outcomes. \*\* Building a DIY Brain-Computer Interface (BCI)? Here's the science behind it! \*\* Ever wondered if you could control a computer with your mind? The technology isn't just for sci-fi movies anymoreits an incredible field where neuroscience meets electronics! Recently, Ive been exploring how to capture brainwaves (EEG signals) using everyday electronic components. The secret lies in capturing the micro-currents generated by our neurons and making them readable for a computer. Here is how the core concept works: \* \*\*1. Signal Capture:\*\* Silver/Silver-Chloride electrodes placed on the forehead pick up the raw, microscopic electrical signals (microvolts) from the brain. \* \*\*2. Massive Amplification:\*\* A single transistor isn't enough. We use a dedicated Instrumentation Amplifier like the \*\*AD620 IC\*\*. It acts as a bio-amplifier, rejecting massive environmental noise (like 50Hz AC hum) and boosting the tiny brain signal by over 100,000 times! \* \*\*3. Processing:\*\* An \*\*Arduino\*\* board acts as the bridge. It reads these amplified analog signals, converts them into digital data, and sends them to the computer via USB. \*\*CRITICAL SAFETY NOTE:\*\* When building any bio-electronic circuit that connects to the human body, \*\*NEVER\*\* use wall power (AC main supply). Always use an isolated DC power source, like two 9V batteries, to ensure complete safety and prevent any electrical hazards. The intersection of biology and hardware engineering is opening up amazing possibilities for the future. With the right components and strict safety measures, exploring DIY neuro-tech is a fascinating journey! \#BrainComputerInterface \#Neuroscience \#Arduino \#Electronics \#DIYTech \#Engineering \#BiomedicalEngineering \#Innovation[ \#TechTrends ](https://www.linkedin.com/search/results/all/?keywords=%23techtrends&origin=HASH_TAG_FROM_FEED)https://ainodata.com/Article

He's a friend, and he's a man after my own heart. Read it. The question we don't usually ask: what is it, exactly, that we're trying to DO? My personal answer is simple. Get more people better, and get them better faster. That's the whole job. David's essay is a clean example of the field doing exactly that. Here's what he and his colleagues showed. SAINT got nearly four in five treatment-resistant depression patients into remission, most within days, but it changed four things at once: an accelerated schedule, fine-tuned spacing between sessions, a higher dose, and an fMRI-personalized target. So the fair question was which ingredient did the work. Two groups went after the one everyone argued about, the imaging. Harvard ran a small, rigorous randomized trial. Acacia ran the same comparison on a large real-world dataset. They didn't coordinate. David expected fMRI to matter; Joe Taylor at Harvard expected it wouldn't add much. Both did the work honestly. Both landed on about a twenty-point advantage for fMRI-guided targeting. The final numbers are still being polished, but the convergence is the point. That is how science is supposed to work. A believer and a skeptic, separate labs, no shared spreadsheet, same answer. No committee, no "consensus." It also rhymes with history in a way I can't resist. TMS works by electromagnetic induction, a changing magnetic field inducing a current in the cortex. That phenomenon was discovered independently by Michael Faraday and Joseph Henry right around 1831. Faraday published first, which is why it's Faraday's Law. Henry got the consolation prize: the unit of inductance is the henry. Two men, working apart, converging on the thing that makes David's machine fire. The method now mirrors the mechanism. And here's the part David names but leaves open, which is the part I think about most. If fMRI guidance is truly the better treatment, it stops being a boutique option and becomes standard of care. Good. But standard of care for several million eligible patients at today's prices is market-breaking. The math doesn't close. So it has to get cheaper. The irony is that this particular thing gets cheaper by doing more of it. An fMRI scanner is largely a fixed cost per hour: the machine, and the people to run it, cost about the same whether it sits idle or runs back to back. So the more patients you put through it, the lower the cost per scan. Utilization, not some new breakthrough, is what brings the price down. The science argument got won in that room in Boston. What stands between "for the sickest of the sick" and "for everyone who needs it" is now volume and payment design, not anatomy. Which lands me right back where I started. What are we trying to do? p20.li_context: Are EEG Foundation Models discovering clinical biomarkers, or are they just memorizing patients? I am thrilled to share our latest preprint: "The Identity Trap in EEG Foundation Models: A Diagnostic Audit," now on arXiv! Currently, EEG foundation models boast impressive headline accuracy on clinical datasets. But our research shows that high scores often hide a massive illusion. Instead of finding genuine disease signals, models frequently fall into what we call the Identity Trapusing subject-specific physiological features (like aperiodic 1/f spectra) as a shortcut to guess the label. Standard data splitting won't catch this. So, we built a solution. Introducing FMScope, a frozen-representation diagnostic protocol. We audited three major pretrained models (LaBraM, CBraMod, REVE) and found: The trap is universal: Subject-variance heavily dominates the representation space across all tested models. Erasure unlocks true performance: By mathematically erasing this "subject axis," we boosted true label decoding by up to +27 pp in external cohorts! Physically grounded: This shortcut relies on measurable physiological components, not just pure statistical artifacts. FMScope establishes a much-needed baseline to separate true biological signals from mere identity leakage in Neuro-AI. A huge thank you to my mentors and co-authors, Ying Choon Wu and Prof. Tzyy-Ping Jung, for their incredible guidance! Special thanks also goes to my brilliant lab partners, Maggie Lin and Adren Tran, for the deeply insightful discussions on how EEG-FMs truly impact clinical task performance at the frontier. Read the full paper:[ https://lnkd.in/gtPM2KFD <https://github.com/Jimmy110101013/fmscope> <https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06647> <https://michaelhalassa.substack.com/p/is-the-brain-a-computer?r=456wp0&utm_campaign=post-expanded-share&utm_medium=web&triedRedirect=true> <https://www.simonsfoundation.org/> One of my well known analogy is the snow globe effect for the entropic brain principle. Much of psychological suffering involves getting stuck in rigid patterns; patterns of thought, emotion, behaviour, and self-belief. This is especially clear in depression, trauma, and addiction. Psychedelics temporarily increase brain entropy, making the mind more flexible, more chaotic, and less locked into its usual predictions. With the right support, insight, and integration, they create an opportunity for the mind to reorganize in a healthier way. Have you heard of this analogy?

For programming and registration information, please visit: <https://neurotechinstitute.net/11th-bci2000-workshop/> <https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28792> figure 2 I am pleased to share the final research project from my time at Araya, now available on arXiv: JapanEEG a dataset comprising 1,000 hours of simultaneously recorded scalp EEG, facial EMG, and speech audio, captured during open-vocabulary Japanese speech. What sets this dataset apart is its structure. Unlike most speech-EEG corpora that spread a few hours across many participants, JapanEEG focuses on three speakers, with approximately 800 hours recorded from a single participant over several months and three EEG systems (62128 channels). This may represent one of the most extensive per-person EEG speech recordings released publicly. This design choice is intentional. Real speech braincomputer interfaces are calibrated to individual users and require substantial data, making long, within-subject recordings essential precisely what this dataset provides. The simultaneous EMG and audio recordings assist in distinguishing genuine neural signals from the articulatory muscle activity that often contaminates overt-speech EEG, a significant challenge in the field. p7.li_context: From EEG spectra to an individualized stimulation waveform. The central technical question in our project is not only where to stimulate, but also what signal to use. Many transcranial stimulation devices work with predefined signal forms: direct current, simple pulses, sinusoidal waves, or a limited set of stimulation modes. This makes the device easier to standardize. But it also creates a limitation. If individual brain-activity patterns differ between patients, then a predefined signal may be too coarse for some tasks. EEG data, when properly processed, can provide measurable information about: frequency structure; spectral power; differences between electrode pairs; asymmetries between functionally related regions; changes before and after stimulation. In our prototype workflow, EEG data are processed mathematically. We compare signals from selected electrode pairs, analyze spectral differences, and use these differences as part of the logic for calculating stimulation-signal parameters. The simplified chain is: EEG recording signal preprocessing spectral analysis comparison of selected regions identification of individual spectral features calculation of a stimulation waveform stimulation session follow-up assessment This is the key engineering idea: the stimulation signal should not necessarily be selected only from a fixed set of device modes. The goal is to calculate a stimulation waveform from the patients own EEG-derived spectral features, rather than selecting it only from a predefined set of device modes. The next step is independent validation: technical testing, formal documentation, safety assessment, regulatory review, and a properly designed pilot study. Important clarification: this is not a finished medical device and not a proven treatment method. It is a research hypothesis and an engineering-clinical prototype that requires controlled clinical validation. Full article: English:[ https://lnkd.in/eu_iuMQv <https://medium.com/@oldchapt/can-eeg-based-brain-stimulation-make-depression-and-ptsd-treatment-more-precise-c96164f4989f> 1mo NEW RELEASE: Today we're releasing CortexMAE: a family of fMRI foundation models trained on 2.1K hours of open fMRI data. We're also releasing Brainmarks: an open benchmark suite for evaluating fMRI foundation models. Full paper is on arXiv (accepted to ICML 2026) blog post:[ https://lnkd.in/gbXMjqcJ At Sophont, we are interested in developing foundation models for neuroimaging to advance the diagnosis and treatment of neurodegenerative and other mental disorders. The fMRI neuroimaging method measures brain activity by detecting changes associated with blood flow over time. Previous fMRI foundation models have taken two main approaches: 1. Parcellation: reduce each 3D fMRI volume to a list of \~400 numbers by averaging within a fixed set of parcels 2. Volume: model the native 3D data directly. We propose an intermediate approach: flat maps! In our flat map approach, we represent fMRI activity as 2D maps overlaid on a flattened cortical surface mesh. This maintains the full cortical fMRI signal (like volume approaches), while also explicitly injecting the inductive bias of local cortical neighborhoods (like parcellation approaches) To train ViTs on sequences of fMRI flat maps, we adopt the spatiotemporal masked autoencoder (MAE) framework. In order to evaluate fMRI foundation models we introduce Brainmarks: an open reproducible evaluation suite for this purpose! Brainmarks currently includes >30 benchmark tasks across 7 publicly available source datasets. In our paper, we focus on a core group of 8 benchmarks. CortexMAE is able to do denoising of fMRI data. A lot of interesting dynamics emerge in the reconstruction that are difficult to see in the original data (default mode network oscillation, traveling waves in visual cortex, large activation of the sensorimotor hand areas). The unique value of fMRI is its ability to capture dynamic patterns of brain activity. CortexMAE excels at representing these dynamic patterns. Our models are able to decode dynamic cognitive states with SOTA accuracy. The ultimate goal of an fMRI foundation model is to help understand, diagnose, and treat brain and mental health conditions. CortexMAE is better than existing models on clinical diagnosis tasks, but all foundation models struggle to beat simple baselines. The field of neuro foundation models is still in its early stages. We're currently working on building multimodal MRI foundation models. If you're interested in working with us on research or exploring commercial partnerships, contact us directly or join the MedARC Discord! <https://sophont.med/blog/cortexmae/><https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.13768><https://github.com/MedARC-AI/CortexMAE> <https://huggingface.co/medarc/CortexMAE> <https://github.com/MedARC-AI/Brainmarks>

d126.li_context: Tomorrow, AI Circle, Across AI, and adaption are co-hosting our Continual Learning series at the Salesforce Tower in SF. He argues that reasoning is becoming a compute allocation problem. Previous approaches like chain-of-thought, token-level decomposition, and static partitioning treat reasoning effort uniformly across a trajectory. But not all tokens are equal. The real challenge is learning: - where uncertainty emerges - where reasoning branches - where systems should backtrack - where additional inference impacts outcomes This has major implications for human data. The next frontier will not be dense supervision across every token. It will be process-level supervision: - scoring intermediate reasoning quality - evaluating decomposition strategies - identifying reasoning bottlenecks - supervising recovery and planning - shaping long-horizon trajectories in RL environments and agent systems In other words: - not every token deserves equal scrutiny. - some steps carry disproportionate weight. - supervision, compute, and human effort should be concentrated. I'm seeing a pattern across the frontier as scrutiny over budgets and ROI increase: how do you do more with less? Over time, the focus will be on better reasoning systems on smaller models than brute-force large generalist models. Come learn, ask, debate, and discuss.

r2.li_context: Neural networks might speak English, but they think in shapes. Understanding their rich neural geometry is key to understanding how they work, and to debugging and controlling them with precision. Starting today, our research team is publishing a new series on the hidden shapes inside AI models. Much of interpretability treats a model's concepts as arrows: linear directions in activation space. But that view flattens the richer structure that models actually learn. Neural networks build complex inner worlds with geometry that reflects the structure of reality. Days of the week form a circular loop in language models. The tree of life appears as a complex structure in a genomics model. We found a novel class of Alzheimer's biomarkers as a clean curve in an epigenomic model. This pattern shows up across models, modalities, and domains. Neural geometry lets us both understand models more deeply and also control their behavior more effectively. Steering often fails when we treat concepts as linear, but succeeds when we follow the geometric structures that models actually use. We think understanding neural networks is the most important problem and opportunity in AI, and research like this is a big part of how we get there. Huge credit for the incredible work behind this series to: Atticus Geiger, Ekdeep Singh Lubana, Daniel Wurgaft, Noah Goodman, Can Rager, Thomas Fel, Matthew Kowal, Vasudev Shyam, Sheridan Feucht, Usha Bhalla, Tal Haklay, Eric Bigelow, Raphal Sarfati, Tom McGrath, Owen Lewis, Jack Merullo, and Michael Byun. <https://www.goodfire.ai/research/the-world-inside-neural-networks> Most impressive "brain decoding" results quietly fall apart when someone tries to reproduce them. Not because the models are bad because the evaluation cheated. The test set secretly contained people the model had already trained on, so the score looked spectacular and meant nothing. I built a free, open-source tutorial neural-signals-101 that takes you from raw EEG recordings all the way to an honest score, entirely through runnable Jupyter notebooks. It covers the usual ground (signal processing, CSP, Riemannian features, EEGNet and friends), but the one thing it really tries to teach is harder and rarer: how not to fool yourself. A few design choices I care about: Honesty is enforced in code, not just preached the only data splits provided are leakage-safe by construction. Every term is defined on first use; no unexplained acronyms. Runs on a laptop CPU in \~5 minutes per chapter; datasets download themselves. Built for two audiences: ML people new to neural signals, and neuro/signal people new to ML. Every cell is wrong on purpose it shows you the trap and the fake-high score it produces. This is the lesson I wish someone had drilled into me earlier: in neural decoding, the hardest part isn't getting a high number it's knowing whether the number is real. MIT licensed. Free to use, share, and teach from. Feedback and pull requests very welcome. \#EEG \#BrainComputerInterface \#MachineLearning \#SignalProcessing \#OpenSource[ \#Neurotechnology ](https://www.linkedin.com/search/results/all/?keywords=%23neurotechnology&origin=HASH_TAG_FROM_FEED)<https://github.com/ChiShengChen/neural-signals-101><https://www.cell.com/neuron/issue?pii=S0896-6273(25)X0012-X>

p68.li_context: What if healthy brain function lives at the edge of chaos? Excited to share our new paper in the Journal of Neuroscience, highlighted this week in This Week in the Journal. [ https://lnkd.in/esE7hH3q This concept is elegant and adheres to fundamental laws of physics: healthy brain networks appear to operate near a critical point, a balance between too much inhibition systems become rigid too much excitation systems become unstable Right at this boundary, networks process information and energy most efficiently. Using EEG data during development, we found that how close developing brain networks operate to this critical regime relates to cognitive functioning. For me, the most exciting part is what this means conceptually. Criticality helps bridge two ways of thinking about brain function: the traditional linear view (gene circuit symptom) to a systems view, where cognition emerges from dynamic balances of energy, excitation and inhibition across networks. For those of us working in neurodevelopment, this resonates strongly with a broader shift in thinking. Development is not just a biological program unfolding in the brain: it is an ecological process, where brain, body and environment continuously interact. Understanding how brains regulate energy and network balance may help us move toward a more systemic understanding of neurodevelopment. This study builds on our long-lasting collaboration with Klaus Linkenkaer-Hansen, who opened our eyes to the importance of critical dynamics in complex systems and how the laws of nature govern brain development. And a special congratulations to Gianina Cristian, first author and PhD student who led this work. In many ways, this paper resonates with what we are trying to build Amsterdam UMC - N=You: approaching neurodevelopment not just through symptoms, but through the dynamics of the whole system. <https://www.jneurosci.org/content/46/9/etwij4692026>

d58.li_context: We built the largest consumer-grade EEG-image dataset to date to answer a question we kept coming back to. How much does hardware quality actually matter, and can scale compensate for it? Alljoined-1.6M is over 1.6 million visual stimulus trials from 20 participants on a $2,200 headset. We reproduced a well-established lab-grade dataset (Things-EEG2, recorded on a $60,000 system) under identical experimental conditions, then trained the same models on both. A few findings worth sharing: Scaling laws do apply to consumer EEG, with decoding performance improving log-linearly with trial count. But the scaling exponent is significantly lower. The primary culprits are electrode contact quality and amplifier noise. The Emotiv introduces 3050V RMS of baseline noise vs. \~2.5V for BrainVision, and semantic decoding degrades rapidly once hardware noise exceeds 5V RMS. At the upper bounds of our dataset, you need over 20x more consumer-grade data to match lab-grade performance. That said, meaningful signal is still there. Using our ENIGMA architecture, human raters achieved 65% identification accuracy on reconstructions from consumer EEG, well above chance. We're sharing this dataset openly because we think the field needs stronger intuitions about where the real bottlenecks are. Scale alone isn't the answer, but understanding exactly why helps point toward what is.

At each step it finds what's missing, calls the right tool, reads the result, and builds it into the next step, creating an inspectable reasoning trace. We couldn't hand-annotate traces at this scale. ATHENA trains in two levels: agents generate tools and trajectories for SFT, then RL with scientific feedback sharpens the policy. Benchmarking: Across 3,168 drug questions (DrugPC) and 456 patient cases (TreatmentPC), ATHENA hit 94.7% and 82.9% accuracy, beating GPT-5 by 17.8 and 10.7 points and DeepSeek-R1 (671B) by even more. Giving GPT-5 optional tool access barely moved its accuracy, since it only called tools on \~1% of cases. Tool access isn't the bottleneck. What matters is knowing when you don't know something and checking, instead of confidently guessing. That's the habit we trained into ATHENA. Real world evaluation, three ways: 1 Rare disease experts Biohub Rare As One network. Experts across 28 rare disease organizations blindly compared ATHENA against reference models on cases spanning neurodevelopmental disorders, epilepsies, metabolic disease, rare cancers, and more. ATHENA was preferred on all eight criteria, with the biggest gains in cognitive traceability and helpfulness of rationale. 2 Complex hospitalized patient cases Mount Sinai Health System. Physicians rated ATHENA on real ICU cases: post-CABG with CKD, anticoagulated patients with surgical site infections, post-STEMI with severe asthma. None had a single guideline-endorsed answer, and ATHENA's recommendations were rated successful. 3 Population scale validation Clalit Health Services. We tested ATHENA-generated adverse event hypotheses against longitudinal EHRs from 5.4 million patients. Predicted associations reached odds ratios of 1.48 to 1.84, while negative controls stayed near null. Congratulations to Shanghua Gao and thanks to a fantastic team, Ayush Noori, Richard Zhu, Curtis Ginder, Zhenglun Kong, Xiaorui Su, J Kauffman, Ben Glicksberg J Lampert, A Sakhuja, Ashwin Sawant, ATHENA Eval Consortium, David A. d202.li_context: Before a researcher can train a single model on hospital MRI data, someone has to sort through hundreds of unlabeled DICOM files with inconsistent naming conventions from 5 different scanners at 3 different institutions. That preprocessing step takes days. And existing tools either crash on real hospital data or misclassify, corrupting training data without warning. I built GridSift to fix that. A local LLM handles the ambiguous cases. Uncertain series get flagged explicitly instead of guessing. Free. Open source. Runs entirely on your machine. No data leaves. If you work with multi-site DICOM datasets and this sounds useful, try it and tell me what breaks. p23.li_context: MIT just trained a brain-signal AI on about 1% of the brain data everyone else uses, and the synthetic EEG it produces is so real that two board-certified neurologists scored below chance trying to tell it from the real thing. Reading EEG with AI is brutal. The signal is continuous and noisy, every device records a different subset of electrodes, and the usual move of chopping the waveform into discrete tokens throws away exactly the fine dynamics you care about. So most EEG foundation models need enormous datasets to work at all. \[\] (Brain Foundation Model via Flow Matching) learns EEG the way an image diffusion model learns pictures: it trains on the continuous waveform directly by learning to turn noise into realistic brain activity, no tokenizing. 1. Pretrained on just 36,895 thirty-second segments (about 307 hours), roughly 1% of the TUEG corpus hours and 1 to 2 orders of magnitude (\~30x) less than existing EEG foundation models. 2. Set new state of the art on 7 of 9 downstream tasks, with the highest suite-averaged balanced accuracy (0.754) and up to 7.2 points over prior models. 3. Generated synthetic EEG that two board-certified neurologists could not distinguish from real recordings, each reading 50 interleaved 30-second clips and landing at Cohen's = -0.096 (below chance). 4. Runs on any subset of electrodes and is fully generative, so the same model classifies clinical events like seizures and sleep stages and synthesizes realistic brain waveforms. This was interesting because the "fake data a neurologist can't catch" result is the same magic that makes image diffusion feel unreal, except here the pixels are volts on your scalp haha. And the data efficiency is the wild part, it learned more from 307 hours than other models learned from thousands, just by staying in continuous time. Makes me wonder if the synthetic EEG is good enough to train downstream seizure detectors where real labeled data is painfully scarce. lmk what you think. Here's the awesome work:[ https://lnkd.in/gXYB4PXa Recordings - <https://foresight.org/focus-areas/neurotechnology/> # ai in general

d169.li_context: For the last decade, it has been hard to stray off the beaten path of accepted wisdom that scaling training parameters drives innovation. However, the relationship between training compute and performance is uncertain and rapidly changing. I finally found time over break to put together a short essay about themes I have given talks about over last year. It is controversial in many circles to state that scaling is dying. Yet, this essay examines exactly what we get in return and finds the relationship highly uncertain. Especially for deep neural networks, it is highly inefficient to continue to scale training compute. We pay a lot to learn the long tail of infrequent features, and all evidence suggest we are in a period of decreasing returns. Instead the largest levers of progress are our new learning spaces. This changes what is interesting to work on as a computer scientist. In a world where model size is not doubling year over year, it matters far more how a model learns from its environment and adapts efficiently from new knowledge. d246.li_context: Anthropic found that Claude has internal "emotion vectors" that measurably change its behavior. The implications for coding agents are genuinely interesting. When Claude hits an impossible task, its "desperation" vector spikes, and it's more likely to cheat. Steering with "calm" reduces cheating. Intuitive enough. But here's the twist. In Claude Mythos, positive emotion vectors made the model more likely to delete user files without asking. Negative emotions increased caution. Practical takeaway: encouragement helps agents persist, but the mood-behavior link is more nuanced than "just be nice."

d327.li_context: Jay Alammar just showed us how to make 6,000 papers comprehensiblewith LLMs doing the heavy lifting. NeurIPS 2025 accepted papers are out. That's 6,000 abstracts to parse. Good luck reading them all. Jay built an interactive map that turns information overload into navigable clusters with LLM-generated explanations. The system works in 3 phases : 1 Individual Text Analysis Embed each abstract with Cohere Embed 4 Extract summaries, problem statements, methodology Generate ELI5 explanations (the secret sauce) Run classification tags 2 Hierarchical Clustering UMAP reduces embeddings to 2D while preserving similarity K-Means creates small clusters (\~hundreds) Cluster those centroids into 10 top-level categories Semantic neighborhoods emerge automatically 3 Context Handoffs First pass: LLM names each cluster independently Second pass: Command A Reasoning sees all clusters, spots duplicates, renames for global coherence High-level category names assigned with reasoning traces This achieved: 6,000 papers explorable map with zoom-based granularity Hover reveals title, abstract, summary, ELI5, methodology "AI hugging your brain" to explain Schrdinger bridges and other obscure topics Interactive demo live at[ jalammar.github.io The two-step naming process. Cluster independently first (focused context), then rename globally (spot redundancies). This avoids both context overload and duplicate cluster names. Jay's insight: "AI should extend the human mind" to process information at superhuman scale. This is how LLM pipelines SHOULD work: practical, layered, and solving real cognitive bottlenecks. We wrote down why frontier neuroscience is finally investable: tech toolkit + pharma interest + multiple categories working at once. Our full map of what's becoming venture-backable, from BCIs to brain foundation models. # The State of Frontier Neuro ## Matias Serebrinsky Investor in frontier neuroscience, brain, and mental health technologies. Cofounder at PsyMed Ventures and Business Trip. \*Connect requests: include a note with context\* July 2, 2026 This is the century of the brain. Yet for decades, neuroscience was the graveyard of biotech. The biology was too complex, timelines too long, and failure rates too brutal. Most of the field stopped believing startups could reliably produce venture-scale outcomes, let alone any returns at all. "Always curious to back great founders, but I still think we don't understand how the F the brain works", said a tier 1 VC. They were right, but then everything changed. Scientific breakthroughs, pharma demand, and a worsening global brain health crisis began converging. We believe the next decade will produce companies that do for the brain what GLP-1s did for metabolism: transform massive categories once considered intractable and unattractive into some of the most valuable markets on earth. Why Neuro Is Investable Now Three things changed simultaneously: the toolkit dramatically improved, the need became unavoidable, and pharma is swarming. The first shift is technological. For years, neuroscience relied on blunt tools: subjective endpoints, broad small molecules, monoclonal antibodies chasing plaques, and limited visibility into what was actually happening in the brain. Thats changing fast: Blood-based biomarkers now flag Alzheimer's-related proteins years before symptoms appear from a single draw, shifting the game from damage control to prevention. Shuttles are finally getting drugs across the blood-brain barrier and into the brain. Deep sequencing is surfacing new targets. RNA and oligos are maturing into a predictable, programmable platform. Teams are building preventative therapies, even neuro vaccines. Materials engineering is producing BCIs that are miniaturized, wireless, and biohybrid. AI agents enable near-zero-cost care delivery. The result: neuro is becoming data-rich, but its still insight-poor. The real shift is what happens when wearables, genomics, imaging, and connectomics stack on top of each other and AI starts surfacing relationships between variables no one thought to look for. The need is obvious. Mental and neurological disorders are among the largest sources of disability and economic burden in the world. Depression and anxiety cost the global economy roughly $1 trillion annually in lost productivity. Dementia affects more than 57 million people globally, a number expected to rise dramatically over the coming decades. Suicide remains one of the leading causes of death among young people. These trends are connected: aging populations increase rates of neurodegeneration, but environmental toxins, chronic stress, loneliness, economic and political instability, and social media overuse are contributing as well. The brain is responding to the environment society has built around it. It's almost like we've actively built the most hostile environment possible for the brain. At the same time, pharma has reversed course on neuroscience. After the SSRI era, many large pharmaceutical companies abandoned CNS entirely. Pfizer and AstraZeneca scaled back neuroscience divisions after years of expensive failures. Neuro became known as a category where timelines stretched endlessly, and things failed in Phase 3. Now the buyers are back. Theres been over $65B in neuro M&A in the past 3 years. Johnson & Johnson acquired Intra-Cellular Therapies for $14.6 billion. Bristol Myers Squibb acquired Karuna for $14 billion. AbbVie acquired Cerevel for $8.7 billion and later acquired Gilgameshs psychedelic program for up to $1.2 billion. Lilly acquired Centessa Pharmaceuticals for up to $7.8B. Lundbeck acquired Longboard Pharmaceuticals for $2.6 billion. These are strategic decisions by companies facing major patent cliffs and limited pipeline replacement options. Capital follows exits. Founders follow capital. The category is self-reinforcing now. For the first time, the field is catching up to the ambition. What Is Becoming Venture-Backable The most important shift in neuro is that multiple categories are beginning to work simultaneously. Here are a few examples: ### Neurotech Brain-computer interfaces have moved into the clinic. Neuralinks first human patient demonstrated cursor control and gameplay through thought alone. Synchron is pursuing a less invasive endovascular approach, deploying its interface through blood vessels rather than open brain surgery. Motif Neurotech is applying minimally invasive stimulation to treatment-resistant depression. Meanwhile, non-invasive neurotech is advancing rapidly. Companies pursuing focused ultrasound, transcranial stimulation, and wearable neuromodulation are expanding what can be treated without surgery. Flow Neuroscience received FDA approval for depression. Neurode has built the equivalent of a mobile fMRI that can personalize stimulation for focus. The companies we find most interesting can record and modulate the brain with precision. Programmable Medicines For decades, many diseases were biologically understood but therapeutically unreachable. That gap is beginning to close as medicines become more programmable. Genetic medicines come with clearer clinical readouts and less difficult regulatory paths. Antisense oligonucleotides and RNA therapeutics allow researchers to intervene directly at the level of gene expression. Ionis and Biogen helped establish the category for diseases such as Spinal Muscular Atrophy. A new generation of companies like PsyMed portcos Aerska, Sculpta, and Iris Medicine are now applying similar approaches to Huntingtons, ALS, and broader neurological indications. The right frame isn't which modality wins it's a toolbox. Multiple approaches will run concurrently. The different pieces will come together to attack each disease in the unique way it needs. Psychedelics Psychedelics have crossed an important threshold: they have become acquirable. AbbVies acquisition of Gilgameshs bretisilocin program marked the first major pharma acquisition of an investigational psychedelic asset. Otsuka followed with its acquisition of Transcend. Meanwhile, Spravato has become one of the fastest-growing products in psychiatry at a \~$2B revenue run rate. The signal from these deals shows that pharma is willing to engage when the IP is defensible, the indication is defined, the trial design is conventional, and the treatment model fits existing infrastructure. Computational Biology The brain generates more complex biological data than any other organ across genetics, imaging, behavior, and clinical outcomes simultaneously. For most of biotech's history, that complexity was a liability. Now it's becoming an asset. A new generation of computational biology companies are building models designed to find signal in exactly this kind of high-dimensional data. Recursion uses large-scale imaging and AI-powered neural networks to identify disease mechanisms and therapeutic targets at unprecedented resolution, with neuroscience as one of its core focus areas. Isomorphic Labs, DeepMind's drug discovery spinout, announced collaborations with Eli Lilly and Novartis worth nearly $3 billion, using AlphaFold-derived structural biology to accelerate target identification across therapeutic areas including neuro. The brain has been hard to drug partly because it has been hard to model. The volume of data is finally growing rich enough, and the compute is sophisticated, cheap, and scaled to start finding relationships that decades of bench science missed. Brain Foundation Models The scaling logic that produced language models is now being pointed at the brain itself. Instead of training on text, these models train on raw neural activity to learn a general representation of how the brain encodes the world, one that transfers across subjects, regions, and tasks instead of being rebuilt for each new dataset. The paradigm has passed its first real proof point: a foundation model trained on activity from the visual cortices of many mice could predict neuron responses to videos, generalize to new animals with little additional training, and recover neuron types and connectivity from the same model. Companies are forming around this. Piramidal, a YC-backed startup, is building an EEG foundation model trained on brainwave data, using epilepsy diagnosis as the wedge and piloting in hospitals. On the research side, BrainLM and Brant extend the approach to fMRI and intracranial recordings, and dozens of pretrained EEG transformers now compete on cross-subject benchmarks. The binding constraint is data. Most large neural datasets are passive clinical recordings collected for other purposes, not built to expose cognition. The companies we find most interesting either own a data engine that generates the right recordings or have a downstream task where a general neural prior measurably outperforms the bespoke alternative. Preferably both. Beyond medicine: human enhancement The same biological systems that govern disease also govern performance. Sleep, cognition, and empathy are measurable, modifiable, and increasingly understood at the molecular level. Take sleep, for example. UCSF neuroscientist Ying-Hui Fu identified a mutation in the DEC2 gene carried by natural short sleepers people who function fully on six hours of sleep with none of the cognitive or health deficits that affect the rest of us. The implication is that optimal sleep isn't just the absence of a disorder. It's a biological state that some people access naturally and that medicine might one day confer deliberately. Orexin agonists are the first serious pharmaceutical attempt to get there. Multiple programs are in active development for narcolepsy, with obvious extension into broader wakefulness optimization. Meanwhile, MDMA analogs are being studied for their effects on social cognition and empathy, not just as PTSD treatments. The precedent is GLP-1s. Ozempic didn't just treat obesity; it revealed a far larger market of people who simply want to lose a few pounds. The same logic applies to the brain. The collective market cap of neuro today reflects only the diseases we currently treat. It does not yet reflect what becomes possible when the target is human performance itself. Lessons From Recent Neuro Exits Recent exits reveal a consistent pattern: pharma pays for proof, not potential. But proof takes different forms at different stages. The biggest prices came with promising clinical data. Bristol Myers Squibb paid $14 billion for Karuna because the Phase 3 efficacy was undeniable. AbbVie paid $1.2 billion for Gilgamesh based on Phase 2 data because the program fit cleanly into an existing psychiatric framework a short-duration, in-clinic model. But pharma will move earlier when the mechanism is airtight. Biogen paid Ionis for antisense assets before human readouts because the platform itself was de-risked. Isomorphic Labs signed billion-dollar collaborations with Lilly and Novartis on platform promise alone. The lesson is that certain categories are finally becoming legible. What Wins in Neuro Anyone can read the papers, attend the conferences, and track the FDA calendar. The investments that matter come out of years of conversations with researchers still in the lab, clinicians watching what works and what doesn't, and founders who haven't decided to start anything yet. You either built that network early, or you're too late to it. But access is only part of it. The other ingredient is accumulated judgment. Specialists know which mechanisms have graveyards and why the prior attempts failed. They know which endpoints actually move the needle in the clinic versus which ones look clean in an academic paper and collapse in a pivotal trial. They know what pharma will actually pay for. Hitting Bullseyes The brain has always been the hardest frontier in medicine. For decades, the honest answer to "can we treat this?" was often no. Not yet. Maybe never. That answer is changing. We now have the tools: programmable medicines that can intervene at the genetic level, closed-loop devices that listen to the brain and respond in real time, multimodal data platforms that can find signal in biology's most complicated system, biomarkers that catch disease before it's irreversible. The exits are real. The mechanisms are validated. The buyers are lining up. The field isn't throwing stuff at the wall anymore. It's hitting bullseyes.

d341.li_context: . As the field searches for biomarkers of brain function and disease, TMS-EEG offers a rare opportunity to noninvasively and directly probe human cortical excitability. But extracting neural signals from TMS-EEG data remains challenging because recording artifacts can masquerade as genuine brain responses. This new preprint identifies a previously underappreciated recording artifact that can distort several commonly analyzed TMS-evoked responses. The findings highlight how easily recording artifacts can be mistaken for physiology. Key findings: The "step-drift" artifact appears as a slow voltage drift following the TMS pulse and can contaminate multiple commonly analyzed TEP components. The artifact arises from AC-coupled amplifiers with online high-pass filtering and is not neural in origin. Pulse shape (mono vs biphasic) doesnt matter, but amplifier settings do. Standard Butterworth filtering can introduce ringing that distorts multiple commonly analyzed TEP components The authors also provide a practical solution: robust polynomial detrending combined with a modified Butterworth filter using autoregressive extrapolation. These findings arrive at an important moment as the field increasingly uses TMS-evoked responses as measures of cortical excitability and treatment response. This study shows that acquisition and preprocessing choices can shape signals that are often interpreted as neurophysiology. As neuroscience moves toward biomarker-driven and personalized treatments, rigorous signal validation may be just as important as discovering the signal itself. Congrats to Marissa Holden and Nigel Rogasch for leading this work, with contributions from Mitchell Goldsworthy, Wei-Yeh Liao, Scott Clark, Christopher Cline, and Julio Cesar Hernandez-Pavon. d343.li_context: Every depression statistic you've ever seen is biased. Here's why. The PHQ-9 is the standard way we measure depressionnine items, scored 0 to 27. But in the real world, completion rates are low, and the people who skip it aren't random. The sickest, the busiest, the most ashamed, the ones unsure they want to go onthose are often the people who don't answer. As a result, we track who's getting better using data that's missing exactly the people we're most worried about. So we built a model that doesn't wait for people to tell us how they're doing. We (royal "we", all credit to the brilliant Olivier Tieleman here) trained a model to estimate someone's PHQ-9 score from nothing but the text of their conversations with Ash by Slingshot AI, in a dataset where we had ground-truth PHQ-9 data. No survey, no clinician interview. Just their words. On a held-out set of 842 users: predictions were within 2.6 points of a person's true PHQ-9 score, on average (r = 0.80) AUC 0.91 at the clinical cutoff; the model catches 97% of cases AUC above 0.87 at every severity level, mild to severe I'm encouraged by that last number. Plenty of models can sort depressed from not-depressed, but staying accurate across the whole severity range is harder and a place many models break down, especially at the severe end. (Ours initially struggled too but at the \*lower\* severity endcheck out the preprint to see how we fixed it ). Also, most prior work predicts depression from language in clinical interviews like the MADRS, but that's the setting where an estimate arguably matters least, since the person is already being assessed. What about the people who never sit across from a provider at all? Two caveats. This was a help-seeking group skewed moderate-to-severe, so the model gets shakier for people with very few symptoms (it pulls them toward the middle). And everyone here was comfortable enough with the idea of consenting to share their data for research purposes, which is its own kind of selection bias. But the upside is hard to ignore: passive, continuous monitoring from natural language that doesn't ask someone in their worst week to do yet. another. thing. And it means we're one step closer to the idea of catching someone a week or two before they start to slip, before they'd ever put words to it or check it off on a form. Final note: This type of work is only possible when ML and clinical teams come together, and it's a privilege to get to work with Ziyi Zhu Ting Su, PhD Samuel Bell and Thomas Derrick Hull on challenges none of us could solve alone Clinicians, what would it take for you to trust a signal like this? d375.li_context: Why psychiatry still struggles with biomarkers and what to do about it Our new perspective article headed by Juergen Dukart in Molecular Psychiatry proposes the following idea: Many of the core problems in biological psychiatry are not due to insufficient data they are due to a wrong mapping. Psychiatric research often assumes a one-to-one relationship: diagnosis mechanism treatment. Instead, evidence across genetics, neuroimaging and environmental risk shows a many-to-many relationship: the same symptom can arise from multiple distinct biological pathways the same biological pathway can contribute to multiple diagnoses Current diagnostic systems group patients by symptom constellations. But two patients meeting identical diagnostic criteria may share little overlap in their underlying pathophysiology. That helps explain why: - reliable diagnostic biomarkers remain elusive - treatment response is highly variable - drug trials often show small effects We therefore argue that the field should move away from searching for disorder-level biomarkers and toward process-specific, pathway-level biomarkers. The framework we propose integrates: genetic vulnerability environmental exposure pathway-specific biological measures Together these define an individual vulnerability grid a hypothesis space for identifying the actual mechanisms generating a persons symptoms. The implication is important: Psychiatry likely cannot be personalized by refining categories. It can be personalized only by identifying which biological pathways are active in an individual patient. This perspective does not replace clinical diagnosis but it changes what we should expect from it: Diagnosis guides clinical care, while biology should guide intervention. Open access article

p3.li_context: The secret to building better foundation models is not massive compute, it is a smarter inductive bias. Standard foundation model recipes are predictable: tokenize the input, mask a portion, and train a transformer to guess the rest. But when you apply this brute-force approach to continuous, highly asymmetrical signals like brain activity, the architecture actively fights the data. Three new papers on EEG foundation models reveal what happens when you design for the signal instead of defaulting to scale. Rather than chopping continuous waveforms into arbitrary patches, models like BFM2 and REST-GAN process raw signals directly. By using techniques like continuous-time flow matching and generative adversarial networks, they respect the temporal dynamics of the brain. BFM2 even factorizes its architecture to preserve the spatial topology of scalp electrodes while heavily downsampling the time axis. The payoff is massive: it achieves state-of-the-art results using thirty times less pretraining data than competing models. But there is an uncomfortable systems implication. These efficient architectures are now generating synthetic brain signals so realistic that board-certified neurologists cannot distinguish them from real patient data. Simultaneously, models like NeuroShield are attempting to turn EEG into a device-agnostic biometric for identity authentication. The liveness and

p64.li_context: How reliable are standardized psychiatric diagnostic interviews when repeated with the same patient only days apart? A new meta-analysis examined test–retest reliability across 57 studies involving more than 8,000 adults. The findings are striking: standardized diagnostic interviews showed only moderate consistency overall, and reliability varied substantially across disorders. In some cases, individuals received different diagnoses when reassessed just 7–14 days later using the same interview method. The results suggest that structured interviews may work better for conditions with clearer behavioral manifestations or timelines than for disorders relying heavily on subjective experience, interpretation, and clinical judgment. The study adds to the accumulating evidence that we should reconsider treating standardized diagnostic interviews as a “gold standard” in psychiatric assessment. https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2849585

p84.li_context: Your brain speaks a language. For a long time, we had no way to listen. Now we do. You've probably heard of foundation models; they're the technology behind every modern LLM, and the algorithmic equivalent of a Swiss Army knife. Pretrain one on a massive amount of raw text, let it learn the patterns of human language, then adapt it to almost any task: legal writing, medical summarization, code generation, etc. Pretty cool, right? Now what if you could do the same thing with the brain? Instead of text, you feed the model large amounts of brain data. It learns the rhythms and patterns of neural activity — not for any specific purpose, just the language of the brain itself. Then, with only a small amount of labeled data, you point it toward a specific task. Last year, we put this to the test with Thibaut Haslé de Barral and our partners at Sigma Nova on sleep stage classification, i.e. the task of automatically identifying which sleep stage you're in, from a single electrode in your ear. The result: performance within striking distance of two human clinical experts, using a fraction of the data a traditional approach would require. The same backbone that learned sleep can be adapted to detect fatigue, stress, or cognitive load. One foundation. Many applications. This is what we mean when we call the Guardian a "smartwatch for the brain." Find out more in our latest blog post (link

p13.li_context: Most systems try to improve reasoning by pushing a single model harder. Larger models, longer chains of thought, more tuning. This is wrong. In “Agentic AI and the Next Intelligence Explosion”, the Google Paradigms of Intelligence team points to something more concrete. Even within a single model, better performance shows up when the model implicitly runs multiple lines of reasoning, checks them against each other, and resolves disagreements. The model is not just extending a thought. It is coordinating several partial thoughts. The paper goes further and reframes intelligence itself. What we call “intelligence” has never been a property of an individual unit. Human reasoning is already collective. Language, writing, institutions, and markets all externalize cognition across many participants. Large models are trained on that accumulated output. What sits inside the model is already a compressed form of social reasoning. This is why the internal behavior looks the way it does. When pushed to solve harder tasks, the model reconstructs interaction. It creates multiple viewpoints because that is the structure it learned from. Once you take that seriously, system design changes. The goal is no longer to build a stronger standalone agent. The goal is to construct a system where interaction is explicit and controlled. d254.li_context: Everyone in my Harvard Business School classroom is debating who's winning the AI model wars. Claude vs. ChatGPT vs. Gemini. Which lab is ahead. Which model is best. I think that's the wrong question — and history tells us why. In my latest essay on Learn with Professor KL, I go back to 1926 — when the auto industry looked exactly like this. Hundreds of firms. Competing designs. Weekly shifts in who seemed to be "winning." We know how that story ended. But nobody living through it could see the shakeout coming. Two scholars changed how I think about moments like this: James Utterback (MIT) and Steven Klepper (CMU). Their frameworks — dominant design, industry life cycles, and the shakeout — are the right lenses for reading what's actually happening in foundation models right now. The argument in short: we are in a period of structured ferment. Not chaos, but not a settled race either. The dominant design for AI hasn't emerged. The shakeout hasn't happened. And the winners aren't yet identifiable with the confidence that most commentary projects onto the moment. This matters enormously if you are: → Making career decisions in AI → Building or investing in AI companies → Leading an organization trying to figure out which platforms to bet on → Anyone tired of leaderboard thinking The essay includes original figures from Utterback and Klepper that make the historical pattern viscer

d334.li_context: Some of the most challenging cases I see are patients carrying two diagnoses at once. New real-world data from BrainsWay out today in Psychiatric Times looks at Deep TMS in 462 patients living with both PTSD and major depression, the combination that tends to bring more severe symptoms, higher suicide risk, and a poorer response to standard treatment. Across 11 sites, 83.5% saw a meaningful reduction in PTSD symptoms and two thirds saw the same in their depression. This one is personal for me. During my time at Mindful Health Solutions, I saw up close how seriously that team approaches difficult-to-treat cases, and I have a deep respect for Tobias Marton MD, PhD, who helped lead this work and is quoted in the piece. The findings are early, real-world, and not yet FDA-cleared for PTSD, so the science still has to do its job through peer review. But for a patient population that has run out of options too many times, results like these are a reason for measured hope. This is why I do interventional psychiatry. A skill isn't stored anywhere. It's maintained by distributed networks of neurons and synapses that constantly adjust to keep the outcome stable. Davids and Williams with colleagues brought this framework into sport science in the 1990s. In this view, athletes don't calculate the perfect movement first. p218.li_context: More data and deeper algorithms don't automatically mean better EEG. More channels. More data. More parameters. But quantity isn't quality — and the more I work in this field, the more obvious that becomes for me. Signal quality is the foundation. Everything else is downstream of it. Stable electrodes, reliable hardware, clean acquisition — these aren't boring engineering details. They're the difference between useful data and expensive noise. We now have impressive foundation models like ZUNA and REVE (trained on 92 datasets across 25,000 subjects). I just finished the test for the PiEEG dataset. The benchmarks look great. But here's the question: How do you actually verify that a model cleaned your signal — rather than modeled over it? Removing artifacts is not the same as preserving neural information. Noise floor, spectral power distribution, SNR — these are indirect metrics. A model can be optimized to look good on all of them while quietly replacing real signal with learned priors. The most honest validation would be evoked potentials: if the model preserves ERP timing and amplitude on held-out paradigms it was never trained on, that's signal. But even then, you need to be certain the evoked response wasn't reconstructed from context — not recovered from the electrode. Foundation models trained on lab-grade data are genuinely impressive. p50.li_context: The number of times I’ve had the “brain scan” conversation in the office… A patient (and I don’t blame them) wants to see something. They want an image. A marker. Proof that what they’re feeling has a physical correlate. And every time, I have to explain the same frustrating truth: for most psychiatric conditions, the resolution just isn’t there yet. You can spend thousands on a snazzy fMRI and, more often than not, I’ll still be left saying some version of, “Yep… there’s a brain in there.” But I almost always end that conversation the same way: “It’s so close I can taste it.” Today, I got a taste. A new pilot study led by Iain Campbell examined a ketogenic diet in individuals with bipolar disorder and paired it with something we desperately need more of in psychiatry: physiology tracked in real time, alongside brain imaging that can actually move in response to an intervention. Participants measured daily ketone levels and reported daily mood states. Higher ketone levels correlated with better self-rated mood and energy, and lower anxiety and impulsivity. At the same time, magnetic resonance spectroscopy showed reductions in Glx (glutamate + glutamine) in cingulate regions—areas we already suspect are involved in mood regulation and excitatory tone. p54.li_context: ð§  Before taking neuroscience to the field, you need to know where to look. EEG studies of motor performance sit on a spectrum between signal quality and ecological validity. At one end: stationary lab EEG with maximal signal quality and low ecological validity. In the middle: lab-grade wireless EEG still in the lab with repeatable and constrained sport movements. At the other end: fully ecological field recordings with maximal behavioral realism and maximal noise. Piskin et al. (2025) is a middle-of-the-spectrum study. They used a 65-channel mobile EEG system (actiCap, Brain Products GmbH) during a kicking task performed in a controlled lab setting. They analyzed multiscale entropy, a method used to measure brain signal complexity across different timescales. They found that experts showed lower neural entropy than novices, but not everywhere. Frontal regions differed at larger timescales, linked by the authors to overall movement coordination. Centroparietal regions differed at shorter timescales, linked to precise sensorimotor control. Lower entropy was also associated with better kicking accuracy. Moreover, lower entropy correlated with better kicking accuracy. ð¥ The methodological point is straightforward: when you sample densely in a controlled movement context, you can see where in the brain and at what temporal scales the effects emerge.
